planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep loamy soil in zone 11–12; Clove Tree is a medium-sized tropical tree native to the North Maluku Islands of Indonesia — the original source of one of the world's most valuable spices — bearing aromatic, red-tinged young leaves and flower buds that are harvested dried (cloves) for use as the whole or ground spice in cooking, baking, dentistry, and traditional medicine worldwide; the most commercially important tree in its native range for centuries.

directions: Plant Syzygium aromaticum (Clove Tree) in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic, deep loamy soil in full sun; requires consistent moisture year-round and high humidity; protect from all frost and cold winds; harvest flower buds (cloves) when they turn from green to light pink but before they open — dried immediately after harvest to the familiar dark-brown clove. Clove is one of the most historically significant spice plants on Earth — the dried, unopened flower buds of Syzygium aromaticum, native exclusively to the North Maluku (Spice Islands) of Indonesia, were so extraordinarily valuable in the medieval spice trade that they were literally worth their weight in gold in European markets. The trade in cloves drove European exploration and colonialism throughout the 15th–17th centuries; the Banda Sea spice islands' control by the Dutch VOC was among the most economically significant monopolies in history. Today, Zanzibar (Tanzania), Indonesia, and Madagascar are the primary producers. The essential oil eugenol (80–90% of clove bud oil) is the source of clove's characteristic powerful aroma; it is used in dentistry as a topical analgesic, in perfumery, and as a food flavoring. The whole spice is used in biryani, mulled wine, pumpkin spice blends, pickling, and traditional medicine worldwide. A large, handsome, aromatic evergreen tree with attractive red-flushed young growth and cream-white flower clusters when not harvested.
sources: Zanzibar Cloves — Tanzania Clove Production Authority historical records
FAO — Syzygium aromaticum clove production statistics

subcategory: Southeast Asian fragrant culinary leaf herb

planting.note: Root stem cuttings or offshoots in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12, or grow as a container plant in zone 8–9; Pandan Leaf (Pandan, Screwpine Leaf, Daun Pandan) is the most important flavoring herb in Southeast Asian cuisine — the intensely aromatic, long, bright-green, strappy leaves provide a distinctive vanilla-coconut-like fragrance and vivid green color to rice, cakes, drinks, desserts, and savory dishes across Indonesia, Thailand, Malaysia, the Philippines, and Vietnam.

directions: Root Pandanus amaryllifolius offshoots or stem cuttings in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; keep evenly moist at all times; in zone 10–12 grow in the ground in a sheltered, partially shaded position; in cooler zones grow in a large container brought inside in winter. Pandan Leaf is the vanilla of Southeast Asia — the green, aromatic leaves of this compact pandanus screwpine are as fundamental to Southeast Asian cooking as vanilla is to Western baking, providing an intensely fragrant, green, vanilla-coconut aroma and flavor to an extraordinary range of foods. The long, stiff, bright-green leaves (tied in knots and simmered in dishes, or juiced and blended into batters) flavor coconut rice (nasi lemak), ketan (sticky rice cakes), chiffon cakes, kaya jam, onde-onde, and countless other Southeast Asian sweets and desserts. Pandan juice (squeezed from crushed leaves) provides a vivid natural green color and strong flavor to pandan chiffon cake and kueh (traditional sweets). The active flavor compound is 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline — the same compound found in basmati rice and jasmine flowers — explaining its distinctive, reminiscent aroma. Unlike most Pandanus, this species is known only from cultivation (never found in the wild) and reproduces entirely vegetatively by offshoots; it is believed to be a sterile cultigen developed in ancient Southeast Asian agriculture. Indispensable for any garden serving Southeast Asian cuisine.
sources: Thailand Institute of Scientific and Technological Research — Pandanus amaryllifolius flavor compounds

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 10–12; Screw Pine is a dramatic, architectural ornamental tree from Madagascar and Mauritius — not a true pine or palm — producing a distinctive spirally arranged rosette of long, stiff, spiny-edged, green leaves at the ends of multiple trunks supported by aerial prop roots, and bold, pineapple-sized orange-red fruit clusters that attract wildlife and provide edible starchy pulp in its native range.

directions: Plant Pandanus utilis in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; highly tolerant of salt spray, coastal conditions, and sandy, nutrient-poor soils once established; water moderately during establishment and reduce thereafter; the aerial prop roots that arch from the trunk to the soil are distinctive and should not be damaged. Screw Pine is one of the most architecturally distinctive of all tropical plants — the multiple, branching trunks supported by tall, arching, aerial prop roots and topped by dramatic rosettes of long, spirally arranged, sword-like leaves (the 'screw' pattern of the leaves gives the plant its common name, though it is unrelated to true pines) create a bold, unmistakably tropical silhouette. Native to Madagascar and the Mascarene Islands, it is widely planted as an ornamental throughout tropical and subtropical coastal regions worldwide for its excellent tolerance of salt spray, sandy soils, and coastal exposure. The large, pineapple-like compound fruits (syncarp) are produced by female trees and consist of densely packed wedge-shaped segments; the starchy base of each segment is edible in Madagascar. The long, flexible leaves are woven into baskets, mats, hats, and thatch (the species name 'utilis' means 'useful'). A magnificent specimen or screen plant for zone 10–12 coastal gardens and tropical landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Pandanus in Florida landscapes

planting.note: Plant tubers in fall 3–4 inches deep in full sun in very well-drained, lean, sandy or gritty, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 7–9; provide complete summer drought; Wreath Nasturtium is a spectacular, large-scale trailing Chilean nasturtium from the Atacama-Andes transition zone — producing masses of brilliant golden-yellow flowers on long, far-spreading stems with distinctive, pale blue-green, glaucous, many-lobed leaves — one of the most striking of all tuberous nasturtiums in cool-winter gardens.

directions: Plant Tropaeolum polyphyllum tubers in fall in extremely well-drained, lean, sandy to gritty soil in full sun; provide absolutely no summer water — summer drought is essential; the plant grows over winter and spring in the cool, moist season, dies back completely in early summer, and re-emerges the following fall. Wreath Nasturtium is one of the largest and most spectacular of the Chilean tuberous nasturtiums — producing wide-spreading, mat-forming or trailing stems that can travel 3–6 feet in a single season, bearing distinctive blue-grey-green, multi-lobed, glaucous leaves that emerge with a smoky bloom and masses of bright golden-yellow flowers along the entire length of the stems. The effect in full bloom is extraordinary: a wide sweep of yellow flowers over a blue-grey foliage carpet, created from a single tuber that develops into a substantial, carrot-like storage root. Native to the Atacama Desert transition zone in Chile and Argentina, where winter rains trigger growth and summer drought forces dormancy, its cultivation requires strict adherence to this rainfall pattern — no summer water whatsoever. Grow in raised beds, scree, or containers moved under cover in summer. The tuber grows very large over successive years (sometimes reaching over a foot long); the large, gnarled tubers are occasionally used as an edible root vegetable in its native Chile, with a pleasant, slightly peppery flavor.

planting.note: Plant tubers in spring 3–4 inches deep in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in zone 8–10; Climbing Alstroemeria is a vigorous tuberous climbing vine from the misty Andean cloud forests of Colombia and Ecuador — related to Alstroemeria but distinctly different in its twining, climbing habit — producing large, pendant, trumpet-shaped flowers in brilliant orange to red-orange with yellow interiors spotted with brown-purple, held in generous clusters at the ends of long, scrambling stems.

directions: Plant Bomarea multiflora tubers in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil; provide support for the twining stems to climb; keep consistently moist through the growing season; in zone 8–9, mulch heavily in winter to protect the tubers from frost. Climbing Alstroemeria is a genus of tuberous, climbing vines closely related to the familiar Alstroemeria (Peruvian Lily) of florists, but with a distinctly different growth habit — the stems twine and scramble through surrounding vegetation to heights of 3–6 feet, decorated with alternate, bright-green, resupinate (twisted) leaves. The large flower clusters at the stem tips bear 10–30 individual pendant, tubular flowers in brilliant orange to deep red-orange (B. multiflora is one of the most vivid-colored species) with a contrasting yellow interior and brown-purple speckling. The genus Bomarea comprises some 120 species throughout the Andes and tropical Americas — an undeservedly neglected group that most temperate gardeners have never encountered despite the extraordinary floral display. Native to the cloud forests of Colombia and Ecuador at elevations of 6,000–10,000 feet, B. multiflora prefers cool to mild temperatures — making it better suited to the mild climates of the Pacific Coast and zone 8–9 Southeast than strictly tropical conditions. Hummingbirds are the primary pollinators in its native range.

planting.note: Plant fleshy tuber clusters in spring 6 inches deep in full sun to partial shade in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 7–10; Yellow Peruvian Lily (Chilean Lily, Orange Alstroemeria) is a vigorous, spreading, tuberous perennial from the Andean foothills and grasslands of Chile and Argentina — bearing abundant, trumpet-shaped, warm-golden-yellow to orange flowers with contrasting dark inner streaks from early summer through fall — the wild parent species from which most modern hybrid Alstroemeria cut flowers derive their warm color range.

directions: Plant Alstroemeria aurea tubers 6 inches deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; water regularly during active growth; deadhead spent flowers to extend blooming; divide clumps every 3–4 years as needed; the plant spreads vigorously by rhizomatous tubers and can become quite extensive. Yellow Peruvian Lily is one of the most widely grown wild Alstroemeria species and the parent (or grandparent) of the majority of modern hybrid cut-flower Alstroemerias in the trade. The species itself is extremely vigorous and free-flowering — producing abundant, 2-inch, trumpet-shaped flowers in warm golden-yellow to rich orange with distinctive brown to maroon inner petal streaks from early June through September. Unlike many Alstroemeria, A. aurea is quite cold-hardy (to zone 7) and spreads enthusiastically in garden conditions, forming large colonies that bloom reliably year after year. The resupinate (upside-down) leaves characteristic of all Alstroemeria are evident in this species. An excellent cut flower with exceptional vase life — stems can last 2–3 weeks in water. Spreads widely enough in fertile soil to become quite assertive — give it room or contain the rhizomatous tubers with buried barriers. In zone 8–10 Gulf Coast gardens, the warm colors and extended bloom period make it particularly valuable for summer borders.
